comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few minutes away, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above was my forty-fourth published book and the result of one of my more improbable discoveries. The Coelbren, for those of you who aren't familiar with it, is a vaguely runic alphabet invented (probably) by Iolo Morganwg aka Edward Williams, a brilliant, batty Welsh poet who contributed a vast amount to the Druid Revival. People had been trying for years to figure out how to use it for divination but nobody could figure out what the individual letters meant. (I've included an example below on theright to show you what it looks like; that's the name of the Welsh province of Ceredigion.)

[IMAGE:1]One afternoon I was in a used book store in central Maryland, right up against the Appalachians, and noticed an odd old book in what was literally the darkest, most obscure corner of the store. I pulled it out and discovered that it was a book on the history of Welsh grammar. Flipping through it, I spotted a reference to a book I'd never heard of by an author I knew well, J. Williams ab Ithel, one of Iolo Morganwg's disciples. I bought the grammar book, went searching for the book by Williams ab Ithel online, found it, downloaded it, and discovered that it contained a nearly complete key to the meanings of the Coelbren letters. The first version of my book on Coelbren followed promptly, setting out a fairly complete system of divination; a second version, greatly expanded and improved with much more material on Coelbren magic and occultism, came out thereafter. If you're interested -- and if you're into Druidry, you should be -- you can get a copy here if you're in the US and here elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed and no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: Review on The Ancient City and Question on Nine Worlds Divination Method
comments.content:
May this week's full moon bring waxing of what you're looking for, and not too much craziness.

To Share: I finally got around to reading Numa Denis Fustel de Coulanges's The Ancient City, which is very interesting from both a historical and religious standpoint, but was perhaps not quite as helpful as I might have hoped for getting a view of what ancient Roman and Greek ancestor worship looked like at a practical level. Still, I've written up my fuller thoughts on it here, for anyone interested: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_thoughts_on_the_ancient_city

To Ask: Fittingly enough for the book featured this week, I have a divination question. So, I know this isn't directly in your wheelhouse, JMG, but I wondered if you might have insight based on analogous methods, or if anyone in the commentariat might have thoughts. In his works on Runecasting, Edred Thorsson describes a method called "Casting Upon the Airts." In this method, you lay out a cloth divided into nine zones, one for each of the Nine Worlds of Norse cosmology. Each of the worlds is associated with certain aspects of the situation being asked about, and which Runestaves land in it tell you what you need to know about those aspects (for example "Midgard" deals with the personality/ego-consciousness, tangible outcomes, and the immediate situation). So far, straightforward enough. In his advice for doing this reading, though, Thorsson recommends that the zones should not be visible on the cloth, or, at a minimum, not their names - I held the former in mind for years now, but checking one source before asking this question, it only said the latter. I'm not sure if he recommends the stricter one in another book, or if this is one of those "you need to re-read the instructions periodically" things, but I figured I'd go ahead with asking anyway.

The following image breaks both versions of that recommendation, but gives a good idea of what the zones on the cloth are. The stricter version I remember would say to cast on a blank cloth divided into these zones in your mind's eye, the less strict version would have the lines, but not the words: https://i.etsystatic.com/12456854/r/il/b938c5/1697354971/il_1588xN.1697354971_kcqr.jpg

Do you have any thoughts on why he might recommend against this? Practice visualizing? Encouraging a more intuitive and less clear-cut method of interpretation? Personal preference?

Thank you again, JMG and everyone here!

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Review on The Ancient City and Question on Nine Worlds Divination Method
replies.content: I have no idea why he would recommend that. In most of the forms of divination I know of, the places into which divinatory symbols are placed are labeled in one way or another; for example, in a horoscope, the houses are outlined and numbered, and in setting out a tarot reading using the Celtic Cross spread, you state the meanings of the positions as you lay out the cards:  "This covers you, this crosses you," and so on. It seems rather odd that Thorson would argue against that.

replies.title: Re: Review on The Ancient City and Question on Nine Worlds Divination Method
replies.content:
Hmm, okay, thanks very much. For what it's worth, I've only ever done this method on a cloth with the zones at least partially shown (I have a cloth with the central circle marked, for example), but I always told myself that was the "training wheels" version, and once comfortable, I'd graduate to doing it right, but in doing such a reading this week, it struck me as something to wonder about, and whether there were a reason besides "always follow magical instructions to the letter until you understand them well enough to start fiddling," so I figured I'd ask.

Thanks again!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Review on The Ancient City and Question on Nine Worlds Divination Method
replies.content:
I did not catch this when I was reading Thorsson's work, and it seems very strange to me. Now, when I first learned runes we used an unmarked cloth, but that was because my teacher only taught face-up/face-down readings. It seems like knowing where one zone ended and the next began would be important, unless he thinks a rune on the border between two zones can influence both?

BTW, Jeff, I got an interlibrary loan of The Seed of Yggdrasil, as your review made me curious. Unfortunately the print is very small (I need new glasses desperately, mine are scratched) and it seems like too much work right now, so I'm going to return it.

--Sister Crow

replies.title: Re: Review on The Ancient City and Question on Nine Worlds Divination Method
replies.content:
His same interpretations do imply that things being on the border should be interpreted as both/bridging/indicating linkages, so he might be encouraging a more intuitive way of judging that, I dunno.

And I'm glad to have sparked your curiosity, but sorry you're finding the book physically unhelpful - I didn't notice that with my edition (the current full-color POD available from the big, slimy river), which makes me wonder if an earlier edition went for a smaller format and squeezed in the voluminous text into too-small a format. On the other hand, I'm blessed with pretty sharp eye-sight and don't yet need reading glasses, so it wouldn't surprise me if it's a problem even with that edition, and I just didn't notice.

If you continue to feel called to it, I hope you find a way to read it that works for you!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Review on The Ancient City and Question on Nine Worlds Divination Method
replies.content:
One practical reason might be so that you can carry the rune staves with you and do divination on any flat, smooth surface such as bare dirt. If you are dependent on a diagram for interpretation, you must have access to the diagram in order to divine properly.

This would be equivalent to the way most people learn to divine with a deck of cards. At first, you have to consult the layout and look up the meanings of the cards in a book or a sheet of keywords. With practice, you dispense with that. I presume that people who consult the I Ching daily recognize every hexagram like an old friend, and eventually don't need to look up the meanings of the moving lines either.

If that's the underlying reason, I don't see why you could not mark the diagram in miniature on the bag or box you carry the rune staves in.

replies.title: Re: Review on The Ancient City and Question on Nine Worlds Divination Method
replies.content:
That makes good sense and might have something to do with it. The only other thing that came to mind after posting is that it might add to the air of mystery and secret powers for the Runecaster to just look at a scattering of chits on a cloth and pull a complex meaning out of it, but that kind of showmanship strikes me as less important to what I want to develop than the ability to make good, solid readings.

I'll have to meditate and yes, even divine, on whether to push for getting comfortable without the markings, to keep using a hybrid approach, or to ignore the advice and go with a more convenient cloth.

Thanks,
Jeff

Hi JMG,

my blessings if you will have them. I have a few small questions:

- is there an effect if an incense stick runs out during a CGD temple working? Or can you just light another and continue?

- Can compulsions from divided will lead to creating personal egregores? I'm thinking cravings for the likes of alcohol, drugs, porn. If they do, can the egregores be combatted?

- I mentioned new upstairs neighbours and their noise recently - while it's better, they do have noisy periods and the building's property manager is willing to ask them if they can make an effort. I thought I'd cast CGD wands for whether this would be for the highest good and got: Judge Elw, RW Carchar LW Bendith fach. While this would seem yes, the Carchar confuses me - doesn't it mean restraint, or have I read this wrong?

Thanks for all you do. I'd like to add my deep thanks for the weekly Corona forum, an oasis of sanity in a nutty world.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) You can just light another stick. It's not a big deal.

2) Yes, and that's much of what addiction amounts to: a self-created egregor charged anew every time you feed the craving. "Combating the egregor," though, is part of the common habit of turning a craving into a scripted melodrama with a prearranged conclusion -- notice you didn't mention "defeating the egregor." Put another way, you won't overcome the habit of dividing your will by reinforcing the division and struggling even harder against yourself! Instead, accept that your will is unclear, manage the craving rather than staging melodramatic fights with it that you have already decided to lose, and focus on understanding the role the craving plays in your emotional life. What are you using it to hide from, or run from? Whose script are you living out by cultivating the habit, and why? What benefit do you get from it?

3) Carchar does indeed mean restraint. If you push too hard right now you'll mess things up and get a reputation as a difficult tenant. Put up with a certain amount of noise, and only make a fuss if and when it gets really loud, or really late at night.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks for 1 & 2. Re 3: While we're both owners not tenants, that's a fair comment. If I ran through my reasoning, could you tell me where you think I went wrong? I figured Elw for Judge was a broadly positive yes to my question, while LW Bendith Fach was success due to another's help (I thought the property manager, who'd sent me a pro forma note that he uses in situations like this that I toned right down as a draft). RW Carchar was what confused me - does it trump the other two figures?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, it doesn't trump the other figures. It just means that you should be careful and not push too hard.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)

comments.title: language of the birds, or, backward and forward anagrammatized
comments.content:
Greetings!

I have a rather specialized question; but first, I'll set the scene.

I've been reading Matte Robinson's The Astral HD, which is a careful study of How the poet HD used esotericism and the occult to develop herself and her poetics. Robinson gets into quite a bit of detail -- though the material on the Golden Dawn is a bit slapdash; anyway, HD does not seem to have had a direct connection with the GD, though she had several indirect connections.

One of her first contacts with modern esotericism seems to have come in her youth, when Ezra Pound, then her lover, introduced her to the work of Yogi Ramacharaka. However, she was haunted by her ancestral connection with an offshoot of the Moravian Brethren -- a secret within the secret, so to speak, which she spent quite a bit of work reconstructing. She also spent some time in spiritualist activities, for a while in connection with Air Marshal Hugh Dowding.

But now I sidle up to the question. She was, later in life, a close reader of several of the works of Robert Ambelain, and Chaboseau's book on the Tarot, significant bits of all of which she incorporated into her poetry (and life).

Nodding over this quaint and curious volume of lore, in particular a chapter in which Ambelain's name appeared many times over each two-page spread, I gradually became aware of a tap-tap-tapping at my mind's door.

It is this: "R. Ambelain" is an anagram of "Abramelin". (If you want to push it, "Robert Ambelain" can turn into "Abramelin Bet Or" (בית אור), but that's a bit of a stretch, eh?)

So I looked around a bit, and couldn't find any reference to R Ambelain as Abramelin.
It seems an obvious enough connection for esoteric puzzle-lovers, but I just didn't find anything. (Perhaps the search engines are not allowed to display such results!)

Have you, or any of the commentariat, heard anything with respect to this?

LeGrand

The book on H.D. sounds worth reading. Discordians will want to know, btw, that if you anagrammatize "backward and forward" you get, among other things, "draw a backward fnord."

replies.title: Re: language of the birds, or, backward and forward anagrammatized
replies.content:
Thanks for the responses.

I'm currently nursing two hypotheses.

First, that the whole thing is so "on the nose" obvious that the lack of mention is a trap devised to weed out the unsubtle who cannot think beyond the first realization. Any public announcement is to be met with a raised eyebrow, a gesture with a cigarette, and a message passed around sotto voce in all the right cafes, so that the hapless discoverer would be forever blackballed from true initiatory orders, and shunted into some suitably dire counter-initiation.

Second, that, inspired by the Plantard maneuver, Ambelain availed himself of advanced Gallic pataphysical technology to seed the whole Abramelin story, in its several manuscript versions, across Europe so that it would appear independently in archives across the continent, to be discovered centuries later.

Applying Occam's hypertrichosis heuristic, I suppose I will go with the one that seems most improbable.

LeGrand

replies.content: Alternatively, R. Ambelain may have been the original Abramelin, having partaken of the elixir of life sometime in the Middle Ages. I'd look for an Alan Bremi, or perhaps a Bela Namir, surfacing as soon as he thinks it's safe..

One of the odd features of physical radiesthesia is that the energy quality, as measured with pendulums, of some alphabets represent the exact nature or meaning of that particular letter or symbol. Hebrew letters have this quality for instance. English does not, there's a gulf of a gap between the energetic quality of a Roman alphabet letter and the reality it represents.

This got me thinking about the Colebren, as its operation works on the level of sound, and doesn't require any of the energetic work to translate it. It's qualities are directly transmitted to human minds without any intermediaries.

What I guess it leads to is a realisation that the process of quantifying everything and forgetting about quality and direct meaning of symbols is more advanced in some human senses than others. Hearing/sound, taste, smell still work largely in the qualitative world.

I've often wondered if the magic implicit in all that is what is largely missing from the western tradition. I also wonder about the symbols themselves, and their meaning, thinking that the reality behind them is on a high level of plane, at least the mental level or above.

In its defense, remember that it's been reassembled from broken and charred fragments more than once.

replies.title: Re: trouble with words
replies.content:
For what it's worth, when scientists try to understand what a female/male distinction would be on the level of causal essences, the two concepts that pop out as central to the causation of sex differences are "anisogamy" and "Bateman's principle". According to the causal understanding that these concepts represent, sex differences in biology have a lot of necessary causal underpinnings that would cause the existence of sex differences to mostly fall apart if the underpinnings were removed. And these underpinnings aren't mostly the sort of thing that one would think to imagine as being relevant if one was just meditating on the ideas of male and female.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anisogamy#Sexual_dimorphism

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bateman%27s_principle

Anisogamy is a possible evolutionary equilibrium in sexually reproducing species having two mating types, such that one mating type produces fewer gametes that are biologically better-provisioned -- defined as "female" -- and one produces much more numerous gametes that are smaller and that compete to reach the better-provisioned gametes -- defined as "male". (The alternative is isogamy: some microorganisms with sexually reproducing phases occur in two or more mutually-fertile mating types, all of which have gametes of the same size.)

Anisogamy is the outcome of a conflict of genetic interests. Each organism genotype would ideally prefer to skip over sexual recombination and direct all the resources of the organism it controls to zygotes which contained 100% copies of just that same genotype (as in parthenogenesis). But in anything but simple microbes, this eventually results in the single-lineage mutational meltdown effect of "Muller's ratchet", as well as a failure to keep up in a coevolutionary arms race against parasites or parasitoids having a faster life cycle and so faster evolutionary selection. Since the genotypes can't get parthenogenesis to work, they have to make the concession of committing to a scheme of sexual reproduction, where each zygote ends up with two genetic contributions of a random half of each parent genotype. But the evolutionary profitability of whichever biological resources are given to the zygote ends up split almost equally between the two contributing half-genomes, regardless of which of the two parent organisms ended up supplying those resources. A given amount of biological resources can be used to provision some number of gametes well, but it can also instead be used to make more half-genomes to stick into additional poorly-provisioned gametes, hoping that the other gamete's provisioning will make up enough of the difference. This results in a conflict of interests between any two would-be parent genotypes, as well as potential conflicts between conspecific genotypes of the same mating type. (For example, under many circumstances, each gamete of one mating type constitutes one additional ticket in an evolutionary sweepstakes to win half the resources held by a gamete of the other mating type.) Under certain circumstances, this conflict can cause evolutionary selection to amplify random pre-existing accidental differences between the sizes and numbers of gametes produced by two different mating types, eventually producing extreme anisogamy and thereby a female/male distinction. This fundamental difference then acts, as per Bateman's principle, as a seed for the different mating, provisioning, and rearing strategies and priorities that differently sexed organisms are directed by their genomes to perform.

These conflicts, that anisogamy or Bateman's principle are outcomes of, are held in place by dynamics on multiple different levels of organization, and this makes it hard to say what the "essence" of a female/male distinction might be. But, for example, the structure of this conflict leads to the suggestion that a male/female polarity might inherently presuppose the existence of a certain baseline fundamental selfishness of the heritable elements of which any putatively female or male thing was composed, or presuppose a tendency toward some analogue of "mutational meltdown" in stagnant closed-off patches of the cosmos, or presuppose a phenomenon of quickly-coevolving parasitism, or presuppose an inherent tradeoff between passing along heritable directives and passing along resources to act on those directives with.

One illustrative partial alternative to a male/female polarity is observed in certain hermaphroditic flatworms, which need to make contact with each other to reproduce (because the alternative would be disadvantageous self-fertilization), but which compete to be the one to fertilize the other and then get away without needing to divert gamete production from sperm to eggs.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pseudobiceros_hancockanus

Although, in other species of hermaphroditic flatworms, the mating interactions are less asymmetric than this.

And then, in some uncommon cases like seahorses or a few species of birds, the behavioral and physical traits typical of "female" and "male" get flipped around from what you would expect from the gamete-level story. Presumably these exceptions are because there are additional biological mechanisms taking effect at stages after the step where an organism produces gametes, that somehow prevent the strategy of lower biological resource investment from being advantageous above the level of producing gametes, even in organisms that are male at the level of what kinds of gametes they produce.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bateman%27s_principle#Sex-role_reversed_species

I'm not sure what to say about how this pattern of partial regularities and exceptions would relate to a proposed (e.g. didn't Gilbert teach something to this effect?) metaphysics where "maleness" or "femaleness" was inherent in most or all distinguished things.

replies.title: Re: Celi and Ceilidh
replies.content: No, none at all. The Irish word ceilidh comes from ceili, "companion, friend" -- a ceilidh is a gathering of friends -- and the Welsh word that comes from the same root is cilydd, which is very rare except in figures of speech like "at ei gilydd," which means "together." The Welsh word Celi, like a lot of Welsh words, comes from Latin -- caelus, "heaven," is its most likely origin.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Causticus wrote:

"for many people words are primarily emotional triggers rather than meaningful content containers. Emotionally-charged words in general are like rocks used to hurl at enemies, rather than to encourage a common understanding."

The roots of this phenomenon are much, much older than the rise of modern media and advertising. In fact, it seems to be hard-wired into the langauge-using part of the human nervous system.

Twentieth-century (pre-Chomskyan) linguistics recognized several different functions of speech, among which two of the most important are the referential fuction (referring to the external world) and expressive function (expressing the speaker's feelings). As early as the 1940s the Dutch linguist A. De Groot stated, as a fundamental principle, that, whenever the two clash, the expressive function always prevails over the referential fuction in shaping the hearer's understanding of any sentence. This used to be called "De Groot's Law," though I doubt that any linguist much younger than I has ever heard of it these days.

Modern politics, of course, milks De Groots Law for all it's worth.

replies.title: (no subject)

1) That's quite common. The first thing to do is to leave some blank space in your divination journal after every reading, and write down what actually happened; then see if you can find, in the divination, the indications that this was going to happen. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't happen to know anything along these lines. Sorry.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't know about nosebleeds specifically, but you might look into red raspberry leaf, and yarrow, for bleeding complaints generally. They have been used since forever, and there should be a lot of historical references, not too hard to find. I use both regularly as a tonic tea (not for nosebleed), and they taste fairly astringent and a little bitter-- the yarrow is particularly unpleasant tasting even in small quantities. I don't mind, but I imagine it was common to dose those along with honey! I have read in some sources that yarrow, used internally like that, can give some people a headache.

I suffered from frequent nosebleeds as a child. Never anything dangerous, but all my pillowcases were stained, and it freaked out my schoolteachers. Alas, I did not cure it with herbs. Middle age seems to have done the trick. I wish I knew the mechanism responsible!

I have used the powder in the bottom of the jar of dried yarrow flowers to apply directly to topical wounds that annoyingly would not stop bleeding, with excellent results-- and fwiw the last one was right on my face, and I was worried it would look terrible. Walked around with a gob of dried herb stuck to it for an hour or so, and looked very silly indeed, but when I gently rinsed it off later, the spot, which had been bleeding profusely... wasn't even red! I imagine its astringent properties must encourage the leaking capillaries there to cinch up. But that's a purely unscientific guess ;) I would not hesitate, myself, to try it similarly on any nosebleed in the more accessible parts of the nostril, but haven't the foggiest idea how it might've been used (or not) for the more high-in-the-nose, or even sinuses, sort. Has it ever been used in extract as a nasal spray, or in a neti pot type thing, I wonder...? Also wondering how unpleasant that would be, as astringent as the stuff is.

I did make a brief foray into the interwebs to try and find you some better sources, but... like all other useful medical information, historical and otherwise, it's not where I remember it. Scrubbed. A Modern Herbal used to be freely available online, and that's... missing now. It was so useful! You can still find Culpeper's complete herbal on internet archive, and that is certainly historical:

https://archive.org/details/culpeperscomplet00culpuoft/page/396/mode/2up?q=yarrow

(here he notes it "restrains violent bleedings and is excellent for the piles"-- and later in the entry recommends it as snuff to induce sneezing, which is probably the opposite of what you'd want for nosebleed!)

comments.title: Trees, part V
comments.content:
I went for a walk in the park recently, and found a large tree stump. It must've been a mighty tree in life, and I could see its substantial roots, which buckled a paved walkway (surely the reason it was cut down). It irked me, seeing such wanton destruction, especially since there's no reason for cement sidewalks in a park anyway (I watched a music video from Japan recently that showed a wooden pathway through a park, which is much better -- I need to visit there, but anyway). I stood on the trunk, and I quietly said, "I'm sorry this happened. If I had it my way, you'd still be thriving here; my species, the humans, can be really dumb sometimes". Shortly after, right on cue, I got the green energy boost I've gotten before from tree-kind, although it wasn't as strong.

I'm wondering, was it the dryad of the former tree who showed me favor, or the nearby trees (which were plentiful)? Also, I wonder if trees can get to know you if you spend enough time near them -- that could be why I got a stronger boost from the great pine I live by, as opposed to the park I visit occasionally. Or maybe it had to do with it being a stump? I'd like to further my understanding.

Where would you place it in relation to other traditions (at a glance it doesn't seem to be explicitly attached to any lineage I'm familiar with, though I'd like to hear your thoughts on that), and what do you think of the work?

2. You recommend sticking with one system and working through it to completion. Do you recommend that a student entirely avoid studying and learning about other traditions while focusing on one, or is the recommendation against the practical working through the system itself?

I agree that focusing on one system for practical work and progressing through it correctly and with dedication is the way to go, but I do find a lot of value in reading about different magical theory and perspectives (especially for meditation themes and comparing ideas!).

Thanks, and I hope your week is well!

replies.title: Re: Initiation into Hermetics
replies.content:
1) Bardon is one of the very few practitioners of classic central European occultism to apper in English. There are scores of similar works in German and in the Slavic languages but not much further west. I haven't worked with Bardon's system but I know people who've gotten very good results with it.

2) Oh, you can read about other systems. Just keep going on the practical work of one system, and also keep studying that system.

replies.title: Re: Initiation into Hermetics
replies.content:
On 1), would you consider Mouni Sadhu in that same vein? When researching Bardon I felt there might be a connection, but I am not anywhere near familiar enough with either of them to speak on it. Just a hunch.

2) Got it.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Initiation into Hermetics
replies.content: No, Sadhu is in the French Martinist tradition. There are some connections but on the whole they're different.

comments.title: The Secret of the Five Rites

replies.title: Re: The Secret of the Five Rites
replies.content:
1-3) I practiced the Rites extensively for three lengthy periods during the forty-odd years I've been practicing magic systematically. I may well return to them again, if I feel the need for the energy boost they provide, but I tend to cycle from one exercise system to another as my needs change.

replies.title: Re: The Secret of the Five Rites

comments.title: Humane tree removal.
comments.content: I took down several trees last autumn. They were volunteers that had grown in my backyard due to years of neglect and apathy by previous residents.  The trees I took down were ones that were in spots where they would cause structural problems in the future and impeded garden plots.  I took them down to the ground but quite a few of them grew new limbs this summer.  In the past I just built a fire on the stump and that seemed to kill them.  Having listened to the conversations here enough I feel like burning them may be cruel. Does anybody know a humane way of killing them?  Thank you-Croatoan

replies.title: Re: Humane tree removal.
replies.content: I'll pass this on to others, because I don't.

replies.title: Re: Humane tree removal.
replies.content:
I'm sorry to post this after hours, and feel free to delete or not pass, but I saw this question just now, and no one had answered it, so, for what it's worth:
I was told by an arborist who was doing work on my property that there is a "stump killer" that can be bought on Amazon for not a lot of money and applied to take care of this problem, which isn't too uncommon. I imagine that would be more humane, although a tree service could also grind the stump down.

replies.title: Re: Barn Hex
replies.content: I would strongly recommend looking into what kinds of hex signs were historically used for various purposes, choosing one, and painting it. Tradition is very useful for the mage.

replies.title: Re: Barn Hex
replies.content:
Might or might not directly help you, but almost certainly contains pointers to what might help more directly: Northern Magic by Edred Thorsson, which contains a bit of discussion of barn hexes and speculation about their possible roots in far older symbolism (like the Runes). Here and elsewhere, I suspect Thorsson might go a bit far in finding direct historical continuity, but the analysis of common elements and principles might be useful nonetheless. Also, as I said, I think he lists at least one source more explicitly about hexes (I'd check, but it's packed away where I can't get to it right now).

k_a_nitz also mentioned a new book on pow wow/braucherei in a recent post, The Red Church by C.R. Bilardi, which is about the same folk magical milieu, but no idea if it covers hexes, and the blurb doesn't say.

Good luck!
Jeff
